coredata.dc:title: A novel synthesis of magnetic and photoluminescent graphene quantum dots/MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co) nanocomposites for catalytic application
coredata.dc:creator: Naghshbandi, Zhwan
Arsalani, Nasser
Zakerhamidi, Mohammad Sadegh
Geckeler, Kurt E.
coredata.dc:description: In recent year, the research is focused on the nanostructured catalyst with increase physiochemical properties. Herein, Different magnetic nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co) with intrinsic photoluminescent and ferromagnetic properties were synthesized by a convenient co-precipitation method. The structure, morphology, and properties of these nanocomposites as well as the catalytic activity of the nanocomposites for the reduction of p-nitrophenol were investigated. The catalytic activity was found to be in the order of NiFe2O4/GQD > CoFe2O4/GQD > NiFe2O4 > CoFe2O4. The sample NiFe2O4/GQD exhibited the best catalytic activity with an apparent rate constant of 3.56 min−1 and a reduction completion time to p-aminophenol of 60 s. The catalysts can be reused by a magnetic field and display good stability, which can be recycled for six successive experiment with a conversion percentage of more than 95%. These results demonstrate that the nanocomposite NiFe2O4/GQD is an efficient catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol compound. Also, the new nanocomposites have shown a significant reduction in the direct and indirect energy bandgaps when compared to pure GQD and the corresponding magnetic metal oxides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
NiFe2O4
CoFe2O4
p-Nitrophenol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nickel ferrite and cobalt ferrite nanoparticles
Synthesis of MFe2O4/GQD (M = Ni, Co) nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation steps of MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites.
NiFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites (a) SEM, (b) TEM and (c) EDX. CoFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites (d) SEM, (e) TEM and (f) EDX.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co), GQD and MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites. (b) XRD pattern of the MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co), GQD and MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites. Magnetic hysteresis loops for the samples (c) NiFe2O4 and NiFe2O4/GQD, (d) CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/GQD.
TGA curves of GQD, MFe2O4 and MFe2O4/GQD.

UV-Visible absorption and PL spectra of the samples (a) GQD, (b) NiFe2O4/GQD and (c) CoFe2O4/GQD. Diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectra for the samples (d) GQD, (e) NiFe2O4/GQD, and (f) CoFe2O4/GQD.

UV-Visible spectra of p-nitrophenol before and after adding NaBH4. Spectra showing the reduction of p-nitrophenol catalyzed by the samples (b) NiFe2O4, (c) CoFe2O4, (d) NiFe2O4/GQD, and (e) CoFe2O4/GQD. (f) Plots of ln(At/A0) vs. time for the reduction of p-nitrophenol with different catalysts.
Reusability of the catalyst for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol.
Elemental analyses of the nanocomposites MFe2O4/GQD from EDX data.
ICP analysis data of the samples NiFe2O4/GQD and CoFe2O4/GQD.
The crystallite size (D), full width at half maximum (B) of the corresponding samples.
Magnetic parameters of the prepared samples.
Eg values from diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS).
Completion time, Kapp, and conversion (%) for reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of prepared samples.
Comparison of the rate constants of a series of nanocatalysts reported in literature with the values obtained in this study for the reduction of p-nitrophenol.

head.ce:title: A novel synthesis of magnetic and photoluminescent graphene quantum dots/MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co) nanocomposites for catalytic application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute for Applied Physics and Astronomy, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute for Applied Physics and Astronomy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology (GIST), and Department of Nanobio Materials and Electronics (WCU), Gwangju, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology (GIST), and Department of Nanobio Materials and Electronics (WCU)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent year, the research is focused on the nanostructured catalyst with increase physiochemical properties. Herein, Different magnetic nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co) with intrinsic photoluminescent and ferromagnetic properties were synthesized by a convenient co-precipitation method. The structure, morphology, and properties of these nanocomposites as well as the catalytic activity of the nanocomposites for the reduction of p-nitrophenol were investigated. The catalytic activity was found to be in the order of NiFe2O4/GQD > CoFe2O4/GQD > NiFe2O4 > CoFe2O4. The sample NiFe2O4/GQD exhibited the best catalytic activity with an apparent rate constant of 3.56 min−1 and a reduction completion time to p-aminophenol of 60 s. The catalysts can be reused by a magnetic field and display good stability, which can be recycled for six successive experiment with a conversion percentage of more than 95%. These results demonstrate that the nanocomposite NiFe2O4/GQD is an efficient catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol compound. Also, the new nanocomposites have shown a significant reduction in the direct and indirect energy bandgaps when compared to pure GQD and the corresponding magnetic metal oxides.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing growth of global industrialization, environment pollution is becoming a ubiquitous and urgent problem. Hazardous pollutants generally result from industries like textile, paper and pulp, printing, petroleum, pharmaceuticals and pesticides, etc. [1]. Among the pollutants, nitro aromatic compounds, especially the nitro phenols, which are found in different waste waters, are major in industrial and agricultural pollutants due to their solubility and stability in water [2]. On the other hand, compounds of the 4-aminophenol family are employed as educts for the synthesis of analgesic and antipyretic drugs, industrial dyes, and some biologically active compounds [3]. Consequently, there is a strong interest to develop an appropriate method for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol in aqueous solution under mild conditions. It is well known that reduction reactions using NaBH4 can be accelerated in the presence of nano-sized catalysts based on metals, metal oxides, and other nanocomposite materials [4,5]. In this context, spinel ferrites (MFe2O4) are utilized as catalysts for the nitro phenol reduction.

Recently, spinel ferrites of the type MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co, Cu, Zn, etc.) have been found to be technologically important magnetic materials due to their salient physico-chemical properties [6,7]. Among these spinel ferrites, NiFe2O4 is one of the most important soft-magnetic ferrite materials due to its ferromagnetic property, low conductivity, low anisotropy field, and chemical stability [8,9]. On the other hand, CoFe2O4 features a high magnetic and thermal stability and anisotropy field [10].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets with a size of less than 10 nm, which exhibit a series of salient properties, e.g., low toxicity, high luminescence, chemical inertness, and easy preparation [11]. In contrast to graphene, which is a zero-bandgap semiconductor, GQDs show a non-zero bandgap and luminescence on excitation [12]. Their outstanding optical and electronic properties are based on their quantum confinement and edge effect [13]. GQDs are prepared by two different methods: by cutting of graphene sheets (top-down approach) or from molecules with aromatic structure, e.g., polycyclic aromatic compounds or fullerenes (bottom-up approach) [12]. Here, a bottom-up approach based on the controlled carbonization of citric acid was employed for the preparation for the GQDs.

In recent years, many research have synthesis magnetic nanoparticles and their nanocomposites and apply them as catalyst nitroaromatic compounds. Ya Tue et al. synthesized PdAu/Fe3O4 nanocomposites for reduction 4-nitrophenol and other nitroaromatic compounds that about 94% reduction of 4-NP was observed in 8 min [14]. Mira V. Parmekar et al. prepared Cu/SiO2@NiFe2O4 in order to reduction 4-nitrophenpl with rate constant 5.41 × 10−3 s−1 [15]. Chaitali Dey et al. synthesized magnetic ferrite nano-catalyst for use in reduction nitroaromatic compounds with rate constant 2.06 × 10−3 s−1 [16]. In addition, recent research have focused on luminescent carbon nanomaterial and their nanocomposites for catalyst application. Jinyan Du et al. prepared Cu-doped carbon dots (CDs) through a one-step hydrothermal for the catalysis activity of 4-NP with rate constant 1.2 × 10−2 s−1 [17]. However, despite the methods used to prepare nanocatalysts, the choosing of a simple and controlled method for fabricate nanocatalysts is still a challenge. We endeavored to fabricate a nanocatalyst that is simple and inexpensive with a better rate constant. Catalysis has transformed the field of industrializing of chemical. Nitrophenols are known to be the most prevalent organic pollutants in waste water generated from agricultural and industrial sources. On the other hand, 4-aminophenol is an important intermediate for the production of analgesic drugs. Therefore, the development of an effective and ecofriendly method to remove these pollutants from water is of great importance [18,19].

In this study, we offer a facile co-precipitation method for the preparation of magnetic MFe2O4/GQD (M = Ni, Co) nanocomposites. The morphology, structure, fluorescence and magnetic properties of the nanocomposites were investigated. In addition, the catalytic properties including the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol was investigated.

Citric acid monohydrate, ferric nitrate ninehydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck (Germany). Other chemicals and solvent were of analytical grade and used as received.

Graphene quantum dots were prepared by the direct pyrolysis of citric acid. In a typical procedure, 2 g of citric acid was placed into a 5 ml beaker and heated to 200 °C [20,21]. After 35 min, the color turned to orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. Then, the orange liquid was added dropwise to 100 ml of basic ethanol (10 mg/ml NaOH) under vigorous stirring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nickel ferrite and cobalt ferrite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
Spinel ferrites were synthesized by the typical hydrothermal method [22,23]: 0.01 mol (4 g) Fe(NO3)3.9H2O, 0.005 mol (1.5 g) Ni(NO3)2.6H2O were dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water and stirred vigorously for 30 min at room temperature. Under continued stirring, the pH of the solution was adjusted to pH ∼ 11 by dropwise addition of 1 M NaOH solution. After stirring for another 6 h, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. The formed precipitate was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times by employing an external magnetic field and then dried at 80 °C for 6 h. The same method was used for preparing the cobalt ferrite nanoparticles. The equations of the reactions for the preparation of the magnetic nanoparticles are given here:
Co
( N
O
3
)

+ 2 Fe
( N
O
3
)

+ 8 NaOH
→
180 ° C,

h

e

+ 8 Na ( N

) + 4
H

O
Ni
( N
O
3
)
2
+ 2 Fe
( N
O
3
)
3
+ 8 NaOH
→
180 ° C,

NiF
e
2
O
4
+ 8 Na ( N
O

) + 4
H
2
O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MFe2O4/GQD (M = Ni, Co) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 500 mg MFe2O4 were dispersed in 25 ml of the GQD solution and then ultrasonicated for 10 min. Afterwards, the mixture was refluxed under argon gas at a temperature of 70 °C and stirred vigorously for 2 h. Finally, the precipitation was washed under external magnetic field with ethanol several times and dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The reaction scheme is shown in Fig. 1 .

The catalytic reduction of nitrophenol was utilized as a catalytic test. To this end, 6 ml of 0.047 mM aqueous solution of p-nitrophenol was placed in a beaker and 10 mg of NaBH4 was added under stirring at room temperature. The color of the solution turned rapidly to bright-yellow due to the formation of phenolate ions. After 1 min, 0.68 mg of the prepared catalyst was added to start the reaction. The dark-yellow color of the solution gradually vanished, indicating the reaction of nitrophenol. The reduction reaction was monitored by UV–Visible spectroscopy by measuring the absorption maxima of nitrophenol and aminophenol.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) powder patterns were taken on a D-500 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The morphology and structure of the nanocatalyst samples were analyzed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, TESCAN MIRA3-FEG) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Leo 912 AB). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by Tensor 27-Bruker FT-IR spectrometer. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded by a Sinco S4100 UV–Vis spectrometer. A spectrofluorometer JASCO FP-6200 was used to record the absorption and fluorescence spectra of samples. The magnetic properties were investigated using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, made by Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Company, applied magnetic field sweeping between ±10,000 Oe) at room temperature. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a thermal analysis system (Linseis L81A1750, German), within a temperature range of 40–700 °C and a heat rate of 10 °C min−1 in air atmosphere. Compositional analyses were carried out on an inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-Shimadzu S7000) for the determination of the metal content in the products.

The magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and the GQDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis of citric acid. The nanocomposites of MFe2O4 and GQDs were prepared through a one-step co-precipitation procedure. The morphology and particle size of the MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites were studied by SEM and TEM and the images are shown in Fig. 2 . From the SEM micrographs (Fig. 2(a, d)), it is evident that spherical nanoparticles were formed with a granularity in the range of 10–25 nm. The average size was found to be 19 nm for the nanocomposite NiFe2O4/GQD and 22 nm for the CoFe2O4/GQD. As shown in (Fig. 2(b, e)), it can be clearly observed that the GQDs are decorated with NiFe2O4 and CoFe2O4 nanocrystals with a size of mostly less than 20 nm. The MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites exhibit a mixed morphology, where the MFe2O4 nanoparticles could be clearly seen to be loaded on the low-contrast GQD layers. The EDX spectra of the prepared MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites are shown in (Fig. 2(c, f)). Accordingly, the presence of the elements Fe, Ni, and Co in the nanocomposites is corroborated. The C, H, Fe, and M ratio of the MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites were obtained by the EDX analysis and are listed in Table 1 . The result of the ICP analyses of the MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites indicated that no alloying occur in the plasma. The concentration of Ni and Co content in nanocomposites of each sample was show in Table 2 . From the ICP result, the atomic ratio of Fe-Ni is 2.04 in NiFe2O4/GQD and Fe-Co is 2.17 in CoFe2O4/GQD.

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs and the nanocomposites MFe2O4/GQD (M = Ni, Co) are shown in (Fig. 3 (a)). For the sample NiFe2O4/GQD, the main absorption band at 600 cm−1 and 895 cm−1 belong to the Ni2+ O and Fe3+ O stretching vibration, respectively. For the sample CoFe2O4/GQD, the absorption band at 575 cm−1 is attributed to the Fe3+ O stretching [23,24]. For the GQDs, The strong and broad peaks at 3430 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic OH stretching. The strong, paired peaks at 1386 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic groups, respectively. After the formation of the nanocomposites, the CO stretching vibration peak shifted, because the carboxylic groups of the GQDs has been converted to carboxylate groups. In addition, all the characteristic peaks of GQDs and the MFe2O4 are observed in the GQD/MFe2O4 nanocomposites, indicating that the GQDs were successfully incorporated into the MFe2O4.

The XRD patterns of the MFe2O4 nanoparticles, GQDs, and MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites are shown in (Fig. 3(b)). The XRD diffraction of the sample MFe2O4/GQD is similar to that of MFe2O4 nanoparticles. MFe2O4/GQD is crystalline, with a [220] peak at 2θ = 32.26, a strong [311] peak at 2θ = 35.65, a [400] peak at 2θ = 43.39, a [511] peak at 2θ = 57.31, and a [440] peak at 2θ = 63.02 corresponding to the cubic spinel structure of NiFe2O4 (JCPDS 10-325) and CoFe2O4 (JCPDS 01-1121). The XRD patterns for the GQDs obtained from the precursor citric acid show a broad peak centered at around 24° [23,25]. The average crystallite size of the MFe2O4 nanoparticles and MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites were calculated from the full width at half maximum of the strongest diffraction peak [311] determined by the Scherrer equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
D =
K λ
B COS θ
where λ is the X-ray wavelength, K is a constant taken as 0.89, θ is the angle of Bragg diffraction, and B is the full width at half maximum. The average crystallite size of the MFe2O4 nanoparticles and the nanocomposites MFe2O4/GQD are compiled in Table 3 .

The room temperature hysteresis loops for the MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co) nanoparticles and the MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites were recorded using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) with an applied magnetic field of −10,000 of 10,000 Oe. (Fig. 3(c, d)) displays the magnetic hysteresis loops of the MFe2O4 nanoparticles and MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites, demonstrating their ferromagnetic behavior. The values of the corresponding magnetic parameters including the saturation magnetization (Ms), coarctivity (Hc) and remanence (Mr) are listed in Table 4 . The hysteresis characteristics of CoFe2O4 shows hard ferrites with high coercive field and magnetization, and the characteristics NiFe2O4 shows soft ferrite with low coercive field and magnetization. The Ms values are 44.85 emu/g for NiFe2O4, 18.32 emu/g for NiFe2O4/GQD, 67.62 emu/g for CoFe2O4 and 47.7 emu/g for CoFe2O4/GQD. The difference in the MS values between the MFe2O4 and MFe2O4/GQD are mainly owing to the introduction of GQDs [26,27].

The thermal stability of the MFe2O4/GQD nanocatalysts were characterized in air atmosphere using TGA. The thermograms of GQD, MFe2O4 and MFe2O4/GQD are shown in Fig. 4 . The sample was heated from 25 to 700 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1. For GQD, three mass loss processes are observed: about 10% mass loss around 100 °C is due to the removal of H2O molecules, and 25% mass loss around 400 °C and 40% mass loss around 700 °C are attributed to the oxidation and degradation of GQD [28–30]. The NiFe2O4 nanoparticles retained 97.45% of the initial mass, the NiFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites retained about 90.5% of the initial mass and the CoFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites retained 87.7% of the initial mass. The main decomposition process of the nanocomposites are in the range of 200–700 °C, indicating the decomposition of GQD and oxidation of NiFe2O4. The TGA analysis indicates that the mass percentages of GQD in NiFe2O4/GQD and CoFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites are calculated as 7% and 8%, respectively.

The optical properties of the GQDs and their nanocomposites were investigated by using UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. A typical digital photograph, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the GQDs and nanocomposite samples are shown in (Fig. 5 (a–c)). The GQDs showed a light-orange color with an excitation wavelength of about 362 nm and a strong emission band at 460 nm. The excitation peak corresponds to the n → π∗ transition of CO and the emission peak corresponds to the π → π∗ transition of CC [31,32]. In the presence of NiFe2O4 and CoFe2O4 the excitation peak was shifted to 255 nm. On the other hand, the emission peaks were shifted to 340 nm for NiFe2O4/GQD and to 336 nm for CoFe2O4/GQD.

The diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption of the GQDs, MFe2O4, and MFe2O4/GQD are shown in (Fig. 5(d–f)). It can be seen that the absorption intensity becomes stronger after loading the GQDs for MFe2O4. The results of the UV–Vis absorption study confirmed that the GQDs in the MFe2O4 could increase the photon absorptive capacity of the sample and keep the inherent band gap of semiconductor. The determination of Eg is possible by applying the Kubelka-Munk method [33] (Eq. (2)):
(2)
F
R
=
1 - R
2
2 R
where R is the reflectance and F(R) is the symmetrical extinction coefficient (α).

The Eg value of a semiconductor can have simple relations for direct and indirect transitions [34–36] (Eqs. (3) and (4)):
(3)
E
g
≈ h ν -
α
2
direct
(4)
E
g
≈ h ν -
α
1 / 2
indirect
The measured band gaps are compiled in Table 5 .

The band gap of the MFe2O4/GQD nanocomposites has been remarkably reduced in comparison to the individual MFe2O4 and GQD nanoparticles. The lower band gap in the nanocomposites causes a rapid release of the electrons. Consequently, the nanocomposites can exhibit better properties in physical phenomena and chemical processes, especially in organic synthesis as effective catalysts.

The reduction reactions of 4-nitrophenol was carried out using NaBH4 as the reducing agent in the presence of the prepared nanocomposites. The reactions were monitored by the time- dependent UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy under ambient conditions. In the UV–Vis spectrum, the absorption maxima corresponding to 4-nitrophenol are centered at 317 nm. After the addition of NaBH4 the peak at 317 nm shifted to 400 nm and the colour of the solution changed immediately from light-yellow to dark-yellow, which indicates the formation of 4-nitrophenolate ions [37,38]. In the absence of the catalyst, the peak at 400 nm remained unchanged even after 10 h. In contrast, after the addition of catalyst, the intensity of the absorption peak at 400 nm gradually decreased with increasing reaction time. Correspondingly, a new absorption peak appeared at 300 nm, demonstrating the formation of 4-aminophenol [3,39].
It was observed that the sample NiFe2O4/GQD showed the highest catalytic activity and the reaction was complete in 60 s, while the reaction time for other the nanocatalyst samples was longer.

The conversion was calculated for all nanocatalyst samples from the UV–visible spectra (Fig. 6 ), using the formula (Eq. (5)):
(5)
( % )
conversion
=
A
0
-
A
t
A
0
× 100
where A0 is the absorbance at time t = 0 and At is the absorbance at any time t. The conversion and completion time for the reduction of the 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol for the samples NiFe2O4/GQD, CoFe2O4/GQD, NiFe2O4, and CoFe2O4 as catalysts are shown in Table 6 .

The apparent rate constant (Kapp) was calculated for all nanocatalyst samples and is defined through the following equation (Eq. (6)):
(6)
Ln
C
t
C
0
=
ln
A
t
A
0
=
k
app

The concentration of NaBH4 was much higher than that of 4-nitrophenol, thus the reaction follows first-order kinetics in relation to the concentration of nitrophenol [40]. The plots Ln (At/A0) vs. time (t) for the reduction of P-NP in the presence of NiFe2O4, CoFe2O4, NiFe2O4/GQD, and CoFe2O4/GQD are shown in Fig. 6f. The values of the apparent rate constants obtained from of the plot of Ln (At/A0) vs. time (t) are given in Table 7 . The rate constant and conversion values of the samples are in the following order: NiFe2O4/GQD > CoFe2O4/GQD > NiFe2O4 > CoFe2O4.

For a quantitative comparison of the NiFe2O4/GQD nanocomposite with those of other catalysts reported previously, the activity parameter (Knor = Kapp/m, where m is the total mass of the catalyst) was introduced and the values are listed in Table 7. Evidently, a superior catalytic activity when compared to the other catalysts could be observed for the NiFe2O4/GQD nanocatalyst. This phenomenon is ascribed to the morphology and synergistic effect of the quantum dots in the nanocomposite.

Different factors such as particle size, redox properties of metal ions, and distribution among tetrahedral and octahedral sites of the cubic spinal lattice influence the catalytic efficiency of ferrites [1,25]. In the catalysis with ferrites, the octahedral sites play a crucial role. When nanocatalyst is added to the reaction mixture, nitrophenol and the borohydride ion (BH4 −) are adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst. Therefore, the nanocatalyst behaves as a medium to transfer electrons from the borohydride ion to the 4-nitrophenol, resulting in the formation of 4-aminophenol. The aqueous medium supplies hydride ions (H−) that are required for the reduction reaction. The catalyst activity of the NiFe2O4 was found to be higher in comparison to the CoFe2O4. After the formation of MFe2O4/GQD, the good electron transport properties of the GQDs facilitate the electron transfer and raise the catalyst rate, which confirms the effect of band gap reduction in the nanocomposites [50,51].

Reusability studies of the NiFe2O4/GQD nanocatalyst were carried out for the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol using the recycled catalyst. In Fig. 7 the reusability of the NiFe2O4/GQD nanocatalyst is shown for the reduction 4-nitrophenol. To this end, the recovered catalyst was filtered off from each run and washed several times, dried, and was checked for six consecutive runs under the same conditions. The conversion of 4-nitrophenol was found to be in the range from 95 to 98% for the six runs investigated.

A series of novel nanocomposites of the type MFe2O4/GQD (M = Ni, Co) have been prepared by a facile co-preparation technique in view of their application in catalysis. The morphology, structure, and the optical and magnetic properties of the nanocomposites were characterized by a variety of methods such as FT-IR spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, TGA, ICP analyses X-ray diffraction technique, PL spectroscopy, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and vibrating sample magnetometry. The catalytic activity of the novel nanocomposites were then investigated for the reduction of p-nitrophenol. The catalytic activity was found to be in the order of NiFe2O4/GQD > CoFe2O4/GQD > NiFe2O4 > CoFe2O4. Interestingly, the nanocomposite NiFe2O4/GQD exhibited the best catalytic activity among the samples studied. The apparent rate constant for this material was found to be 3.56 min−1 and the reduction completion time to p-aminophenol was 60 s. In comparison to values reported in literature for this reaction (Table 7), the values reported here are significantly higher (minimum 1.56 and maximum 406 times). These results suggest that the nanocomposite NiFe2O4/GQD is a promising catalyst for the reduction of nitro aromatic compounds and various environment applications.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile reduction of nitrophenols: Comparative catalytic efficiency of MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Cu, Zn) nano ferrites
Gold-supported magnetically recyclable nanocatalysts: a sustainable solution for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in water
Facile protocol for reduction of nitroarenes using magnetically recoverable CoM 0.2 Fe 1.8 O 4 (M= Co, Ni, Cu and Zn) ferrite nanocatalysts, RSC
Functionalized graphene/Fe3O4 supported AuPt alloy as a magnetic, stable and recyclable catalyst for a catalytic reduction reaction
